h1Title: Laryngopharyngeal Reflux: Symptoms, Causes, Diagnosis, and Treatment
heroOverview: Laryngopharyngeal reflux (LPR) is backflow of gastric contents — acid, pepsin, bile, and gas — into the larynx and pharynx, producing mucosal damage and symptoms without the heartburn or esophagitis characteristic of gastroesophageal reflux disease (GERD). LPR accounts for approximately 10% of ENT clinic presentations and is implicated in 50-60% of patients with chronic hoarseness, voice fatigue, and chronic throat clearing. Unlike GERD, affected individuals are often unaware of gastric reflux because the laryngopharynx lacks the esophageal mucosal defense mechanisms and pain fibers. Diagnosis is clinical using the Reflux Symptom Index and laryngoscopic findings; treatment with dietary modification and twice-daily proton pump inhibitor therapy reduces symptoms in 60-80% of patients over 2-3 months.

keyStats.prevalence: Estimated 10% of ENT clinic consultations involve LPR as a contributing diagnosis; 50-60% of patients with chronic hoarseness have LPR on laryngoscopy evaluation
keyStats.demographics: Affects adults of all ages; peak in the 4th-5th decade; slightly more common in women; significantly higher in professional voice users (singers, teachers, call-center workers)
keyStats.avgAge: Onset typically 35-55 years; pediatric LPR presents differently — with feeding difficulties, stridor, and laryngomalacia — and is managed by pediatric ENT
keyStats.globalCases: Population-based studies suggest LPR symptoms affect 10-20% of Western adults at any given time; formal diagnosis is made in a minority given non-specific symptom presentation

definition: Laryngopharyngeal reflux (ICD-10: J06.0, included under LPR/laryngitis from reflux) is defined as the retrograde flow of gastric contents past the upper esophageal sphincter into the larynx, hypopharynx, oropharynx, and potentially the nasopharynx and tracheobronchial tree. The primary injurious agents are pepsin (active at pH below 7 and potently reactivated from mucosal tissue by non-acid reflux) and acid; bile acids and trypsin are additional contributors in duodenogastroesophageal reflux. The laryngeal and pharyngeal mucosa is far more sensitive to reflux than the esophageal mucosa: as few as 3 reflux events per day reaching the larynx produce inflammatory change, compared with 50 or more events required to cause esophageal symptoms in GERD. This explains the absence of heartburn in up to 60% of LPR patients, earning the clinical label 'silent reflux.' The condition is diagnosed and managed primarily by otolaryngologists using clinical symptom scoring (Reflux Symptom Index, RSI) and laryngoscopic examination (Reflux Finding Score, RFS), with input from gastroenterologists when esophageal pathology is suspected. LPR must be differentiated from allergic and vasomotor causes of chronic throat symptoms, which have overlapping presentations.

typesClassification.description: Gastric acid is the primary injurious agent; responds most reliably to proton pump inhibitor therapy. Nocturnal recumbent reflux episodes are typical, reaching the larynx when the upper esophageal sphincter pressure is lowest during sleep.

Reflux at pH 4-7 containing pepsin, bile acids, and other injurious non-acid components. Not suppressed by PPIs; requires dietary modification, alginates, and lifestyle changes. Increasingly recognized as responsible for PPI-refractory LPR cases.

Gaseous reflux without liquid content; manifests as belching, throat clearing, and globus sensation. Laryngoscopy may show less overt erythema than liquid acid LPR. Treated primarily with dietary fiber control and behavioral intervention.

primarySymptoms: Chronic hoarseness or voice change — the most common single symptom, arising from laryngeal mucosal edema, vocal fold erythema, and posterior commissure pachydermia causing incomplete glottic closure.
Chronic throat clearing — a reflexive attempt to remove the sensation of thick mucus or foreign body in the throat; the act itself causes vocal cord trauma and can perpetuate the irritation cycle.
Globus pharyngeus — the sensation of a lump, fullness, or something stuck in the throat that is present between swallowing rather than during it; reported by 60-70% of LPR patients.

Post-nasal drip sensation — thick mucus at the back of the throat that patients attribute to the sinuses; in LPR, this is often refluxate-triggered mucus hypersecretion rather than true nasal drainage.
Chronic cough, particularly dry and occurring without respiratory infection, triggered by laryngeal reflex arc activation from minimal acid contact with the laryngeal mucosa.
Voice fatigue — vocal endurance deteriorates earlier than in unaffected individuals; professional voice users notice reduced projection and increased effort to maintain pitch and volume.

Dysphagia or odynophagia — difficulty or mild pain on swallowing solids or liquids; caused by posterior laryngeal and hypopharyngeal edema when LPR is severe; warrants endoscopic exclusion of malignancy.
Throat burning or soreness without a clear infective precipitant, particularly after meals or in the morning on waking after nocturnal reflux episodes.
Heartburn is absent in approximately 40-60% of LPR patients, distinguishing the condition clinically from typical GERD where retrosternal burning is the dominant complaint.
earlyWarningSigns: RSI (Reflux Symptom Index) score above 13 on the validated 9-item questionnaire — this clinical threshold identifies LPR with 55% sensitivity and 83% specificity in published validation studies.
Voice hoarseness that is consistently worse on waking in the morning and improves through the day — the reverse of muscle-fatigue hoarseness patterns, suggesting nocturnal reflux.
Chronic cough lasting more than 8 weeks without a clear respiratory cause after a normal chest X-ray and pulmonary function test.
Globus sensation lasting more than 4 weeks in an adult — while usually benign, it warrants laryngoscopy to exclude posterior glottic pathology and pharyngeal mass.
emergencySigns: Progressive dysphagia (difficulty swallowing worsening week by week) with weight loss — requires urgent endoscopy to exclude hypopharyngeal or esophageal carcinoma before attributing symptoms to LPR.
Blood in saliva (hemoptysis or hematemesis) in a patient with chronic throat symptoms — must exclude laryngeal, pharyngeal, or esophageal malignancy urgently.

Sudden-onset stridor or respiratory distress in a patient with chronic throat symptoms — subglottic stenosis from long-standing LPR-induced granuloma or subglottic edema is a rare but serious complication.
Unilateral fixed vocal cord with hoarseness and choking — requires urgent laryngoscopy and imaging to exclude a laryngeal or thyroid mass, as LPR alone does not fix the vocal cord.

causes.cause: Lower esophageal sphincter (LES) and upper esophageal sphincter (UES) dysfunction
causes.description: LPR requires failure of two sphincteric barriers: the lower esophageal sphincter allowing gastric contents into the esophagus, and the upper esophageal sphincter failing to prevent upward migration into the laryngopharynx. The UES has a baseline pressure of 40-100 mmHg and 'catches' most esophageal reflux; transient UES relaxations during swallowing, belching, or sleep reduce this protection. In LPR patients, the number of daily UES relaxations permitting laryngeal contact is increased compared with controls.

Pepsin, the primary proteolytic enzyme in gastric secretions, is active below pH 7 and can be internalized by laryngeal epithelial cells. Once inside, pepsin can be reactivated by intracellular acidification from any subsequent acid micro-reflux events, causing ongoing mucosal injury independent of the initial pH of the refluxate. This explains why non-acid reflux still causes LPR damage and why PPIs that suppress acid but not pepsin have only partial efficacy.

causes.cause: Dietary and behavioral factors promoting reflux
causes.description: Foods that reduce LES pressure or stimulate gastric acid production — fatty and fried foods, caffeine, alcohol, chocolate, peppermint, and carbonated beverages — are established dietary triggers. Eating within 3 hours of lying down, large meals, and high body mass index all increase reflux frequency and severity. Tight clothing and exercise immediately after meals also contribute.

Excess visceral adiposity increases gastric intraluminal pressure, promoting reflux. Each 1-unit increase in BMI above 25 is associated with a 1.2× increase in gastroesophageal reflux; the effect on LPR-specific symptoms is proportional. Weight loss of 10% body weight significantly reduces LPR symptom scores in overweight patients.

causes.cause: Professional voice use and throat-clearing behavior
causes.description: Singers, teachers, clergy, actors, and call-center workers sustain prolonged vocal effort that promotes laryngeal dryness and increases sensitivity to reflux-related injury. Habitual throat clearing, which many LPR patients adopt to manage globus sensation, traumatizes the vocal cords and perpetuates edema, creating a symptom-perpetuation cycle independent of ongoing reflux.

riskFactors.description: Increased abdominal pressure promotes LES relaxation and reflux. Weight loss of 5-10% body weight reduces LPR symptom scores significantly; bariatric surgery patients show substantial reduction in LPR symptoms as BMI falls.

Regular intake of coffee, alcohol, chocolate, fatty foods, carbonated drinks, and spicy foods reduces LES tone and increases gastric acid secretion, directly promoting reflux events that reach the laryngopharynx.

Meals within 3 hours of recumbency allow gastric contents to reach the UES while the stomach is still full. Large meal volumes increase gastric pressure. Dietary pattern change alone reduces LPR symptom scores in 20-30% of patients without medication.

Teachers, singers, and call-center workers have documented higher rates of LPR on pH-impedance testing compared with the general population. Sustained vocal effort both increases laryngeal sensitivity and is compromised by mucosal edema from reflux.

A sliding hiatal hernia displaces the LES above the diaphragmatic hiatus, reducing the anti-reflux barrier from two zones (LES plus diaphragmatic crura) to one. Hiatus hernia is present in 30-40% of patients with GERD and contributes to LPR in those with combined esophageal-laryngeal symptoms.

Nicotine reduces LES tone acutely and chronically, increases gastric acid secretion, and impairs esophageal motility, all promoting reflux. Smoking is an independent risk factor for both GERD and LPR and significantly worsens laryngeal mucosal healing.

Calcium channel blockers (amlodipine, nifedipine), nitrates, theophylline, and anticholinergics reduce LES pressure. Aspirin and NSAIDs promote gastric acid production. Review of medications with prescribing physicians is an important component of LPR management.

affectedDemographics: Adults in the 4th to 6th decades: peak clinical presentation; LPR-associated vocal changes become more pronounced with age-related laryngeal mucosal thinning.
Professional voice users (teachers, singers, actors): prevalence in voice clinic populations is 40-60%; LPR causes disproportionate occupational impairment in this group.
Overweight and obese adults (BMI ≥30): increased intra-abdominal pressure is a direct mechanical cause; LPR prevalence correlates with BMI across population studies.

Pediatric population (infants and young children): LPR in infants presents as feeding difficulties, regurgitation, stridor, cough, laryngomalacia, and recurrent croup; managed by pediatric ENT and gastroenterology.
diagnosisOverview: Diagnosis of LPR is primarily clinical and laryngoscopic, with objective ambulatory pH-impedance monitoring reserved for treatment-refractory cases. The Reflux Symptom Index (RSI), a validated 9-item self-report questionnaire with scores 0-45 (one score per symptom, 0 = no problem, 5 = severe problem), serves as the primary screening tool; a score above 13 is consistent with LPR. Flexible or rigid laryngoscopy visualizes the laryngeal findings scored using the Reflux Finding Score (RFS), an 8-item validated endoscopic scoring tool; RFS above 7 supports the diagnosis. Key laryngoscopic findings include posterior commissure hypertrophy (pachydermia), arytenoid erythema and edema, subglottic edema, thick endolaryngeal mucus, and diffuse laryngeal erythema. Contact granulomas of the vocal process represent a specific sequela of repetitive LPR injury to the posterior larynx. GERD must be distinguished: heartburn, esophagitis, and Barrett's esophagus are esophageal manifestations not typically present in LPR. When symptoms are refractory to 3 months of PPI therapy, ambulatory 24-hour pH-impedance monitoring of the hypopharynx (dual-probe, with the proximal probe at or above the UES) remains the gold standard for confirming pathological LPR events. Pepsin salivary assay (Peptest) is a non-invasive diagnostic tool available in some centers, detecting pepsin in saliva or throat secretions as a proxy for reflux exposure.

diagnosticTests.purpose: A validated 9-item patient-completed questionnaire (0-45 scale) that screens for LPR; score >13 supports the diagnosis
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: You score 9 symptoms from 0 (no problem) to 5 (severe problem). Takes 2-3 minutes. Completed in the waiting room before your ENT appointment. The score guides whether laryngoscopy and empirical treatment are warranted.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Direct visualization of the larynx, posterior commissure, arytenoids, and vocal folds to score the Reflux Finding Score (RFS >7 supports LPR)
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A slim flexible camera is passed through one nostril after local anesthetic nasal spray. Takes 5-10 minutes in the ENT clinic. Images are recorded. No sedation is needed. Mild throat discomfort during passage.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Gold-standard objective measure of acid and non-acid reflux events reaching the hypopharynx; used when clinical diagnosis is uncertain or treatment has failed
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A thin catheter is passed through the nose and positioned with two sensors — one in the lower esophagus and one above the UES. Worn for 24 hours while eating normally and keeping a symptom diary. Removed the next day. Results analyzed by a gastroenterologist.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Non-invasive lateral-flow test detecting pepsin in saliva or sputum as a biomarker of reflux exposure; sensitivity 87%, specificity 74% in published studies
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Collect a saliva sample into a provided tube at a time of symptoms. Send to the laboratory. Results within 5-7 days. Available in some specialist ENT and gastroenterology centers.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Evaluates swallowing function in patients with LPR-related dysphagia; identifies aspiration and pharyngeal motility abnormalities that co-exist with laryngeal inflammation
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: You swallow different textures of food and drink while a flexible nasopharyngoscope records vocal cord and pharyngeal movement in real time. Takes 20-30 minutes. Performed with a speech and language therapist.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Evaluates for esophagitis, Barrett's esophagus, hiatal hernia, and excludes esophageal or gastric malignancy when LPR symptoms overlap with GERD or when dysphagia is present
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Performed under light sedation. A flexible camera is passed through the mouth. Takes 10-20 minutes. You rest for 1-2 hours afterward before discharge. A small biopsy may be taken to assess for Barrett's esophagus or eosinophilic esophagitis.

treatmentOverview: Treatment for LPR is stepped, starting with behavioral and dietary modification, adding pharmacological acid suppression, and escalating to surgical anti-reflux repair in refractory cases. The International Society for Diseases of the Esophagus (ISDE 2019) and European Laryngological Society recommend twice-daily proton pump inhibitor therapy (30-60 minutes before meals) for a minimum of 2-3 months as first-line pharmacological treatment when the RSI and RFS support LPR. Twice-daily PPI outperforms once-daily dosing for LPR, achieving symptom reduction in 60-80% of patients, compared with once-daily which is largely effective in GERD but inadequate for LPR. The most efficacious PPIs for LPR are omeprazole 20-40 mg, esomeprazole 40 mg, or lansoprazole 30 mg, each given twice daily. Antacids and alginates (Gaviscon Advance) form a viscous raft above the gastric contents that physically blocks reflux reaching the UES; they are effective as adjuncts or as primary treatment for non-acid and weakly acidic LPR not responsive to PPIs. The alginate raft strategy is the primary non-acid reflux treatment. Dietary modification alone — eliminating coffee, alcohol, chocolate, fatty foods, carbonated beverages, and late meals — reduces LPR symptom scores by 20-30% without medication and is mandatory in all treatment plans. Laparoscopic anti-reflux surgery (fundoplication) is considered when pharmacological treatment is insufficient after 6 months, when pH-impedance confirms pathological upright LPR, or when non-acid reflux is the predominant mechanism. The LOTUS trial and REFLUX trial demonstrate that fundoplication controls LPR-associated laryngeal symptoms in 70-85% of surgical candidates. Voice therapy with a speech and language therapist (reducing throat-clearing behavior, vocal hygiene) is a necessary adjunct in patients with significant voice symptoms.

medicalTreatments.name: Proton pump inhibitors twice daily (omeprazole 20-40 mg, esomeprazole 40 mg, lansoprazole 30 mg)
medicalTreatments.description: Taken 30-60 minutes before breakfast and before the evening meal to suppress both daytime and nocturnal acid secretion. A 2-3 month trial is the minimum adequate duration for LPR; laryngeal mucosal healing is slower than esophageal healing. PPIs do not suppress non-acid reflux or pepsin.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Symptom improvement in 60-80% of RSI-positive LPR patients at 3 months on twice-daily dosing; once-daily dosing inadequate for LPR (only 30-40% response)

medicalTreatments.name: Alginate antacid (Gaviscon Advance 10 mL after meals and at bedtime)
medicalTreatments.description: Sodium alginate plus potassium bicarbonate forms a viscous gel raft above the gastric contents, reducing the acid pocket and physically blocking proximal migration to the UES. Most effective for postprandial and upright LPR; particularly useful for non-acid reflux and as an adjunct to PPIs.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Significant RSI reduction versus placebo in the ISAC trial; particularly effective for postprandial symptoms; adjunct to PPI improves outcomes by additional 15-20%

medicalTreatments.name: H2-receptor antagonists (ranitidine equivalent: famotidine 20-40 mg at bedtime)
medicalTreatments.description: Added to PPI regimen to suppress nocturnal acid breakthrough; useful when nocturnal symptoms dominate. Taken at bedtime as a separate agent from the twice-daily PPI doses, targeting residual nocturnal acid secretion.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Adding H2RA to PPI reduces nocturnal acid breakthrough from 70% to 30%; modest additional symptom benefit in some LPR patients

medicalTreatments.name: Voice therapy (vocal hygiene program)
medicalTreatments.description: Managed by a speech and language therapist; addresses habitual throat clearing (replacement with a silent swallow), hydration optimization (steam inhalation, frequent water sipping), voice use habits, and stress-related vocal behaviors that maintain laryngeal irritation cycles.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Reduces throat-clearing frequency by 60-70%; RSI improvement of 3-5 points independent of acid suppression; essential in professional voice users

medicalTreatments.name: Baclofen (GABA-B agonist, 5-10 mg three times daily)
medicalTreatments.description: Reduces transient lower esophageal sphincter relaxation frequency, decreasing both acid and non-acid reflux events. Used as an adjunct in PPI-refractory LPR where non-acid reflux is confirmed on pH-impedance monitoring.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Reduces postprandial reflux events by 40-50% versus placebo; modest clinical symptom improvement in trials; side-effect profile (drowsiness, nausea) limits widespread use

surgicalOptions.name: Laparoscopic Nissen fundoplication
surgicalOptions.description: 360° wrap of the gastric fundus around the lower esophagus reinforces the LES and diaphragmatic anti-reflux barrier. Indicated for PPI-refractory LPR confirmed on ambulatory pH-impedance monitoring, or where non-acid reflux is predominant. Eliminates both acid and non-acid reflux.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Controls LPR laryngeal symptoms in 70-85% of surgical candidates; dysphagia risk 5-15%; superior to continued medical therapy in patients with abnormal DeMeester scores confirmed objectively

surgicalOptions.name: Transoral incisionless fundoplication (TIF with EsophyX)
surgicalOptions.description: Endoscopic fundoplication performed through the mouth without incisions, recreating the LES valve using full-thickness sutures. Less invasive than laparoscopic surgery; suitable for patients with small hiatal hernia and moderate reflux who prefer to avoid surgery.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Eliminates daily PPI use in 60-75% of patients at 5 years in sham-controlled trials; inferior to laparoscopic fundoplication for severe GERD but suitable for selected LPR patients

alternativeTreatments.name: Alkaline water (pH >8.0) and plant-based diet
alternativeTreatments.description: A pilot RCT (Zalvan et al. 2017) showed that a pH >8.0 alkaline water diet plus Mediterranean-style plant-based diet achieved RSI reductions equivalent to PPI therapy at 6 weeks. Mechanism proposed as alkaline inactivation of pepsin. Further larger trials are needed, but the dietary approach is safe, evidence-supported in early data, and beneficial for general health.

alternativeTreatments.name: Throat coat herbal tea (deglycyrrhizinated licorice root)
alternativeTreatments.description: Observational data suggest mucilaginous herbal preparations coat the laryngopharyngeal mucosa and reduce irritation from refluxate. Used as a symptomatic adjunct; insufficient RCT data to recommend as standalone therapy, but no harm at recommended doses.

whySeeSpecialist: An otolaryngologist should assess any patient with unexplained chronic hoarseness lasting more than 3 weeks, chronic throat clearing with RSI above 13, globus sensation persisting more than 4 weeks, or dysphagia requiring exclusion of malignancy. A speech and language therapist is essential if voice symptoms are prominent. Gastroenterology referral is warranted for PPI-refractory cases or when GERD with esophagitis or Barrett's esophagus is suspected.
doctorSelectionGuide: Choose an otolaryngologist with a dedicated voice clinic or laryngology subspecialty interest for complex LPR presentations; laryngologists have advanced expertise in flexible and rigid laryngoscopy, videostroboscopy, and combined voice and reflux management. For surgical candidates, seek a foregut surgeon experienced in both Nissen fundoplication and TIF procedures who performs pH-impedance monitoring to confirm objective reflux before operating.
hospitalCriteria: ENT outpatient clinic with flexible laryngoscopy and video documentation
Upper GI endoscopy suite for esophagoscopy and biopsy
Voice clinic with speech and language therapist and videostroboscopy
Laparoscopic surgical service experienced in fundoplication
keyFacilities: GI motility laboratory for pH-impedance testing
Foregut surgery center for anti-reflux surgery
preventionStrategies: Maintain a healthy body weight (BMI 18.5-25); each 10% weight reduction reduces reflux symptom scores significantly, acting through reduced intra-abdominal pressure.
Avoid eating within 3 hours of lying down; allow gastric emptying before recumbency to reduce nocturnal and supine reflux events reaching the larynx.
Eliminate or substantially reduce coffee, alcohol, carbonated beverages, fatty and fried foods, chocolate, and peppermint, which are the most potent LES-relaxing dietary triggers.
Elevate the head of the bed by 15-20 cm (6-8 inches) using bed risers or a wedge pillow to reduce nocturnal gravity-assisted reflux to the larynx.
Stop smoking — nicotine reduces LES tone within 30 minutes of each cigarette and is an independent cause of LPR exacerbation.
lifestyleModifications: Take PPIs 30-60 minutes before breakfast and before the evening meal for full acid suppression during the post-meal acid surge periods.
Replace throat-clearing reflex with a silent dry swallow; habitual throat clearing traumatizes the vocal cords and maintains laryngeal mucosal edema.
Sip water frequently throughout the day to maintain laryngeal mucosal hydration; steam inhalation (facial steam bowl or steamy shower) 10 minutes daily reduces laryngeal dryness.
Avoid tight waistbands and restrictive clothing that increase intra-abdominal pressure; wear loose clothing after meals.
Avoid strenuous exercise — particularly core-loading activities (sit-ups, heavy lifting) — immediately after eating, as increased intra-abdominal pressure during digestion promotes reflux.
Reduce portion sizes and eat three moderate meals rather than two large ones; avoid snacking close to bedtime.

recommended: Alkaline-forming and low-acid foods: bananas, melons, apples, pears, root vegetables, whole grains, and lean proteins that do not stimulate excess gastric acid or reduce LES tone
Non-carbonated plain water, herbal teas (chamomile, ginger in small amounts), and alkaline water (pH >8.0) which may inactivate pepsin in the laryngopharynx
Small, frequent, low-fat meals to reduce gastric distension that promotes transient LES relaxations
Plant-based foods rich in fiber that maintain gastric emptying and reduce reflux frequency
avoid: Coffee and tea (caffeine reduces LES pressure and stimulates gastric acid secretion)
Alcohol (reduces both LES and UES pressure; a potent LPR trigger even in small amounts)
Carbonated beverages (CO2 increases gastric pressure and promotes reflux episodes)
Chocolate, peppermint, and fatty foods (reduce LES tone and delay gastric emptying, prolonging gastric acid exposure)
Citrus fruits, tomato-based products, and spicy foods (increase acid load and directly irritate already inflamed laryngopharyngeal mucosa)

exerciseGuidelines: Regular moderate aerobic exercise supports weight management, which is the most powerful lifestyle intervention for LPR. Avoid exercising immediately after meals; wait at least 90 minutes. High-impact activities (running, aerobics) with a full stomach increase reflux events through diaphragmatic displacement. Low-impact activities (walking, yoga) are preferred during the first hour after eating. Swimming is well-tolerated. Abdominal-compression exercises (sit-ups, planks) after eating should be avoided.
dailyManagement: Take morning PPI 30-60 minutes before breakfast and evening PPI 30-60 minutes before the evening meal — timing is critical for adequate acid suppression.
Adhere to the dietary restriction list consistently, not selectively; even occasional dietary triggers can perpetuate laryngeal inflammation that takes weeks to settle.
Use the RSI questionnaire monthly to track symptom response to treatment; a drop in score below 13 at 12 weeks indicates adequate treatment response.
Attend voice clinic follow-up if vocal symptoms are present; videostroboscopy at 12 weeks confirms improvement in vocal fold edema and guides whether to continue or escalate treatment.
Complete a minimum 2-3 month trial of twice-daily PPI before concluding treatment failure — laryngeal mucosal healing is significantly slower than esophageal healing.
prognosis: LPR responds to combined dietary modification and twice-daily PPI therapy in 60-80% of patients over 2-3 months, with full RSI normalization in approximately 50%. Non-acid and weakly acid reflux, confirmed by pH-impedance monitoring, responds less well to PPI alone and requires alginate therapy, dietary change, or anti-reflux surgery. Voice changes from laryngeal edema resolve more slowly than throat symptoms — typically 3-6 months of treatment is required before significant improvement in voice quality is noticed. Contact granulomas of the vocal process, a specific complication of LPR, resolve with PPI therapy in 60-80% of cases over 3-12 months; the remainder require endoscopic removal or transoral thyrohyoid injection. Long-standing untreated LPR is associated with leukoplakia, subglottic stenosis, and — controversially — an increased risk of laryngeal squamous carcinoma in heavy smokers with concurrent LPR. These complications justify investigation and treatment rather than watchful waiting in symptomatic patients.
recoveryTimeline: Throat-clearing and globus sensation typically improve within 4-8 weeks of dietary change and twice-daily PPI therapy. Hoarseness resolves more slowly — 8-12 weeks for mild to moderate laryngeal edema. Posterior commissure hypertrophy on laryngoscopy may persist for 3-6 months even after symptom resolution. If symptoms have not improved after 3 months of twice-daily PPI plus dietary modification, objective pH-impedance monitoring is required before extending treatment.
complications: Contact granuloma (vocal process granuloma) — a benign inflammatory mass on the posterior vocal fold from repeated trauma of acid-induced coughing and throat-clearing; may cause hoarseness and globus; resolves with PPI in 60-80% but may require endoscopic removal.

Subglottic stenosis from long-standing laryngeal inflammation and fibrosis — rare but causes progressive inspiratory stridor and exercise limitation; requires laser dilation or surgical laryngotracheal reconstruction.
Laryngeal leukoplakia — white mucosal plaques from chronic irritation with low malignant potential; requires surveillance laryngoscopy and biopsy as some lesions undergo dysplastic transformation.

Chronic dysphonia and vocal cord scarring from persistent mucosal inflammation — impairs vocal fold closure and vibration quality; may cause permanent voice change if LPR is not treated early in professional voice users.

Recurrent laryngospasm — involuntary vocal cord closure triggered by minimal reflux events causes sudden choking episodes and frightening temporary dyspnea; responds to PPI optimization and behavioral management.

supportResources.name: British Voice Association
supportResources.description: Patient and professional resources on voice conditions including LPR, voice therapy, and finding a voice-qualified speech and language therapist in the UK.

supportResources.name: American Academy of Otolaryngology — Voice, Swallowing, and Speech
supportResources.description: ENT patient information on laryngopharyngeal reflux, hoarseness, and throat conditions from the US otolaryngology professional organization.

supportResources.name: International Foundation for Gastrointestinal Disorders (IFFGD)
supportResources.description: Provides evidence-based patient education on reflux disorders including LPR, dietary guidance, and medication information.

confusedWithConditions.name: GERD (Acid Reflux)
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: GERD (gastroesophageal reflux disease) primarily affects the esophagus with heartburn, regurgitation, and esophagitis; heartburn is present in over 80% of GERD patients. LPR affects the larynx and pharynx with hoarseness, throat-clearing, and globus; heartburn is absent in up to 60% of LPR patients. GERD is diagnosed by endoscopy; LPR by laryngoscopy. Both may coexist in the same patient.

confusedWithConditions.name: Vocal Cord Nodules
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Vocal cord nodules are fibrous callus formations on the middle third of the vocal folds from phonatory trauma, causing a consistent breathy or rough voice quality. LPR causes diffuse laryngeal erythema and posterior commissure disease rather than focal midcord nodules. Videostroboscopy distinguishes the two; both can coexist in professional voice users.

confusedWithConditions.name: Vocal Cord Paralysis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Vocal cord paralysis causes a breathy, weak voice from failure of glottic closure due to nerve injury (recurrent laryngeal nerve). LPR causes hoarseness from mucosal edema. On laryngoscopy, a paralyzed cord is fixed in a paramedian or lateral position; in LPR, cord movement is full with posterior commissure and arytenoid changes. Laryngoscopy immediately distinguishes the two.

confusedWithConditions.name: Chronic Sinusitis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Chronic sinusitis causes true post-nasal drip from excessive sinus secretions that drain posteriorly, causing throat discomfort, cough, and sneezing. LPR causes a false post-nasal drip sensation from refluxate-triggered mucus hypersecretion and direct laryngeal irritation. Nasal endoscopy, sinus CT, and laryngoscopy are required to distinguish the two; both conditions frequently co-occur.

relatedConditions.name: GERD (Acid Reflux)
relatedConditions.relevance: GERD and LPR share the same underlying reflux mechanism but differ in predominant injury site — esophagus versus laryngopharynx; both frequently coexist in the same patient.

relatedConditions.name: Vocal Cord Nodules
relatedConditions.relevance: LPR is a recognized precipitating factor for vocal cord nodules in professional voice users; treating LPR reduces nodule formation and aids resolution.

relatedConditions.name: Vocal Cord Paralysis
relatedConditions.relevance: Both conditions cause hoarseness and require laryngoscopy to differentiate; LPR occasionally co-exists with paralysis and must be treated to optimize voice rehabilitation.

relatedConditions.name: Chronic Sinusitis
relatedConditions.relevance: Chronic sinusitis and LPR frequently co-occur in patients with chronic upper airway symptoms; distinguishing true post-nasal drip from LPR-related laryngeal mucus secretion is clinically important.

faqs.question: What is laryngopharyngeal reflux (LPR)?
faqs.answer: Laryngopharyngeal reflux is the backflow of stomach contents into the larynx and throat, causing mucosal damage without the heartburn typical of acid reflux. It is often called 'silent reflux' because up to 60% of patients have no heartburn. LPR causes chronic hoarseness, throat-clearing, globus sensation, and chronic cough.

faqs.question: What are the symptoms of LPR?
faqs.answer: Common LPR symptoms include chronic hoarseness, frequent throat-clearing, a sensation of a lump in the throat (globus), post-nasal drip sensation, chronic cough, and voice fatigue. Heartburn is absent in up to 60% of cases. Symptoms tend to be worse on waking and after meals, and gradually improve through the day.

faqs.question: How is LPR different from GERD?
faqs.answer: GERD primarily affects the esophagus and causes heartburn, regurgitation, and esophagitis. LPR affects the larynx and throat, causing hoarseness, throat-clearing, and globus without heartburn in most cases. GERD is diagnosed by endoscopy; LPR is diagnosed by laryngoscopy and the Reflux Symptom Index. Both can occur together in the same person.

faqs.question: Why do I have LPR symptoms but no heartburn?
faqs.answer: The larynx and throat are far more sensitive to stomach acid than the esophagus. As few as 3 reflux events reaching the larynx per day cause significant inflammation, while 50 or more are needed to produce esophageal symptoms. This is why LPR is called silent reflux — gastric backflow damages the throat without the patient being aware of it.

faqs.question: How is LPR diagnosed?
faqs.answer: LPR is diagnosed using the Reflux Symptom Index questionnaire (score above 13 suggests LPR) and flexible laryngoscopy assessing the Reflux Finding Score (above 7 supports the diagnosis). For cases not responding to treatment, 24-hour pH-impedance monitoring with a probe at the upper esophagus is the gold-standard objective test. No single test confirms LPR in all cases.

faqs.question: What is the best treatment for LPR?
faqs.answer: Twice-daily proton pump inhibitors (30-60 minutes before breakfast and dinner) combined with dietary modification and avoidance of trigger foods achieves symptom improvement in 60-80% of LPR patients over 2-3 months. Alginate antacids (Gaviscon Advance) after meals add benefit, particularly for non-acid reflux. Voice therapy addresses throat-clearing and vocal hygiene.

faqs.question: How long does LPR take to get better?
faqs.answer: LPR improves more slowly than GERD because laryngeal mucosal healing takes longer than esophageal healing. Throat symptoms such as globus and throat-clearing typically improve within 4-8 weeks of twice-daily PPI and dietary changes. Hoarseness from vocal fold edema takes 8-12 weeks or longer to resolve. A minimum 2-3 month treatment trial is needed before assessing response.

faqs.question: What foods should I avoid with LPR?
faqs.answer: The main dietary triggers to avoid are coffee, alcohol, carbonated drinks, chocolate, peppermint, fatty and fried foods, citrus, and tomato-based products. These foods reduce lower esophageal sphincter tone or increase gastric acid, promoting reflux. Eating small meals, avoiding eating within 3 hours of lying down, and elevating the head of the bed also reduce symptoms.

faqs.question: Do proton pump inhibitors work for LPR?
faqs.answer: PPIs work for acid-predominant LPR and achieve symptom improvement in 60-80% of patients, but they do not suppress non-acid reflux, which contains pepsin and bile acids that also damage the larynx. Twice-daily dosing before meals is required — once-daily dosing has only 30-40% efficacy in LPR. Alginates are added when non-acid reflux is suspected or response is partial.

faqs.question: Can LPR cause chronic cough?
faqs.answer: Yes. LPR is one of the three most common causes of chronic cough, alongside asthma and post-nasal drip. Minimal acid contact with the larynx activates a reflex arc causing a dry, persistent cough without phlegm. The cough may be the only symptom of LPR. It responds to twice-daily PPI therapy in 60-70% of cases when LPR is confirmed.

faqs.question: Is LPR causing my hoarse voice?
faqs.answer: LPR is implicated in 50-60% of patients with unexplained chronic hoarseness. Stomach acid and pepsin backflowing onto the vocal folds cause mucosal swelling, redness, and posterior commissure thickening that disrupt normal vocal fold vibration. Hoarseness from LPR is typically worse in the morning, improves through the day, and is associated with throat-clearing and globus sensation.

faqs.question: Can LPR be cured permanently?
faqs.answer: LPR can be controlled very effectively with dietary modification, twice-daily PPIs, and lifestyle changes; most patients maintain symptom remission with ongoing dietary adherence. Surgery (laparoscopic fundoplication) controls symptoms in 70-85% of selected patients and can reduce or eliminate medication dependence. Recurrence is common if dietary triggers and lifestyle factors are resumed after stopping treatment.

faqs.question: What is the globus sensation in the throat from LPR?
faqs.answer: Globus pharyngeus is the sensation of a lump, ball, or something stuck in the throat between swallowing. It is present in 60-70% of LPR patients and arises from laryngeal mucosal edema and posterior pharyngeal inflammation from refluxate irritation. It typically does not cause pain on swallowing. Laryngoscopy and trial of PPI therapy are the recommended next steps.

faqs.question: Does weight loss help LPR?
faqs.answer: Yes. Weight loss is one of the most effective interventions for LPR. Excess abdominal fat increases intra-abdominal pressure, which promotes reflux by relaxing the lower esophageal sphincter. Losing 5-10% of body weight significantly reduces reflux frequency and LPR symptom scores. Bariatric surgery patients show substantial reduction in both GERD and LPR symptoms.

faqs.question: Can children get LPR?
faqs.answer: Yes. Pediatric LPR presents differently from adults — with feeding difficulties, regurgitation, stridor, laryngomalacia, recurrent croup, and sleep disturbance in infants, and chronic cough, hoarseness, or throat-clearing in older children. It is managed by pediatric ENT and pediatric gastroenterology teams. Diagnosis requires age-appropriate laryngoscopy and usually an impedance study.

metaDescription: Silent reflux (LPR) causes hoarseness and globus without heartburn. RSI diagnosis, twice-daily PPI therapy, dietary triggers, and anti-reflux surgery reviewed.
